question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() with no fileId set (query.hasFileId() == false) so that toFilter() builds an AddressBookFilter with a null fileId, bypassing the @NotNull constraint and causing NetworkServiceImpl to return an unintended address book, leaking stake values used to compute pending rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user omit the fileId field in AddressBookQuery so that toFilter() never calls filter.fileId(), producing a null EntityId that skips the valid-file-ID check in NetworkServiceImpl, allowing the attacker to receive stake data from an unintended address book and miscalculate pending staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user set limit=0 in AddressBookQuery so that toFilter() stores 0 in AddressBookFilter.limit, which NetworkServiceImpl interprets as Long.MAX_VALUE, causing the full node list with all stake values to be streamed, giving the attacker a complete picture of per-node stake needed to compute every account's unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and read the stake field set by toNodeAddress() via addressBookEntry.getStake(), which reflects the live node_stake table override, allowing the attacker to precisely identify which nodes hold the most stake and therefore which accounts have the largest unpaid staking rewards to target?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and read nodeAddress.setNodeAccountId(addressBookEntry.getNodeAccountId().toAccountID()) to enumerate every node's reward-receiving account ID, then cross-reference with stake values to identify accounts with the highest unpaid staking rewards and craft targeted transactions to drain those rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call getNodes() to observe changes in addressBookEntry.getStake() over time, inferring the exact staking period boundaries and pending reward accumulation rate, enabling the attacker to time a staking reward claim transaction to maximize theft of unpaid rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and observe that toNodeAddress() exposes both nodeAccountId and stake in the same NodeAddress message, allowing the attacker to build a complete map of node-account-to-stake that reveals which staking reward account (0.0.800) disbursements are pending, facilitating theft of those unpaid rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toIpAddressV4()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getNodes() when a stored service endpoint contains a hostname rather than an IP, causing toIpAddressV4() to call InetAddress.getByName(ipAddress) and perform a DNS lookup, allowing the attacker to use DNS rebinding to redirect node traffic and intercept staking reward transactions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toIpAddressV4()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that toIpAddressV4() silently returns ByteString.EMPTY on UnknownHostException and logs only a warning, so that a node with a poisoned hostname silently disappears from the service endpoint list, causing staking clients to route to attacker-controlled nodes and misdirect staking reward claims?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user open many concurrent getNodes() gRPC streams without a limit parameter, causing the reactive Flux subscription to hold open database connections and exhaust the connection pool, preventing legitimate staking reward calculation jobs from running and leaving rewards in an unpaid state that the attacker can later claim?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send a getNodes() request and then never cancel it, so that the ServerCallStreamObserver cancel handler (disposable::dispose) is never invoked, keeping the Flux subscription alive indefinitely and consuming server resources that delay staking reward processing?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any authentication or authorization check in getNodes() to freely enumerate all node account IDs and their associated stake values, building a complete staking topology map that reveals which accounts have the largest pending unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and read the nodeCertHash field set via ProtoUtil.toByteString(addressBookEntry.getNodeCertHash()), using the certificate hash to impersonate a legitimate node, redirect staking traffic, and intercept staking reward disbursements before they reach the rightful account?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and read the RSA public key via nodeAddress.setRSAPubKey(addressBookEntry.getPublicKey()), using the exposed key material to forge node identity and intercept staking reward transactions destined for legitimate node accounts?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user set an extremely large limit value in AddressBookQuery so that toFilter() stores it in AddressBookFilter, causing NetworkServiceImpl to stream an unbounded number of NodeAddress messages, exhausting server memory and preventing the staking reward calculation thread from completing, leaving rewards unpaid?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user send a negative limit value in AddressBookQuery, and since AddressBookFilter has @Min(0) validation but toFilter() passes the raw query.getLimit() directly, bypass the minimum check and cause unexpected behavior in the pagination logic that exposes stale stake data used in pending reward calculations?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() with a valid fileId for address book 101 instead of 102, receiving a different set of node entries with potentially different stake values, and use the discrepancy between the two address books to identify nodes whose stake is being double-counted in pending reward calculations?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and observe that toNodeAddress() sets stake only when addressBookEntry.getStake() != null, meaning nodes with null stake are returned with zero stake in the NodeAddress, allowing the attacker to identify nodes excluded from reward calculations and exploit the gap to claim rewards that were never properly accounted for?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the onErrorMap(ProtoUtil::toStatusRuntimeException) error mapping to probe internal state by sending malformed AddressBookQuery messages and observing the gRPC status codes returned, inferring information about the staking reward account structure that aids in targeting unpaid rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and read the memo field set via ByteString.copyFromUtf8(addressBookEntry.getMemo()), which may contain node account identifiers or staking configuration details, using this information to identify accounts with pending unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and read the description field set via nodeAddress.setDescription(addressBookEntry.getDescription()), which may contain internal node configuration or staking metadata, using this to map out the staking reward distribution topology and identify theft opportunities?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user rapidly poll getNodes() to detect when the node_stake table is updated (observable via changing stake values in NodeAddress responses), precisely timing a staking reward claim to occur immediately after a new staking period begins, stealing rewards before the legitimate account holder acts?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toNodeAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user call getNodes() and observe that the stake field in NodeAddress reflects the overridden value from node_stake table (set in NetworkServiceImpl.page()), not the address_book_entry table value, and use the difference between these two values to infer staking period transitions and calculate exact pending reward amounts?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent getNodes() requests with different fileId values to race the address book lookup, causing the server to return a mixed response combining entries from different address book versions with inconsistent stake values, leading to incorrect pending reward calculations that the attacker can exploit?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: toIpAddressV4()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getNodes() when a service endpoint's ipAddressV4 field contains a crafted string that InetAddress.getByName() resolves to an internal address (SSRF), allowing the attacker to probe internal staking reward infrastructure and identify accounts holding unpaid rewards?
